name: Tawa
description: Tawa is the Hopi sun god and the creator of the First World. He is considered the most important deity in the Hopi pantheon, responsible for bringing light and warmth to the world, allowing life to flourish. Tawa resides in the sky and is associated with the east, from where the sun rises each day. He is the embodiment of the sun's power and its vital role in sustaining all living things. Tawa is not typically depicted anthropomorphically in Hopi art, but rather represented by the sun itself, radiating energy and life. He is closely linked to the concept of balance and harmony in the world, and his daily journey across the sky symbolizes the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. He worked with Spider Woman (Kokyangwuti) in creating the first people from clay, animating them with his breath and the warmth of the sun. Tawa's influence extends to agriculture, as the sun's energy is essential for the growth of crops. He is revered through prayers and ceremonies that honor the sun's life-giving properties and maintain the balance between the natural and supernatural realms. He is seen as a benevolent and nurturing force, providing the necessary conditions for the Hopi people to thrive in their desert environment. His role is crucial in the Hopi creation myth, and his continued presence ensures the continuation of life on Earth.
subtitle: The Hopi Sun God and Creator

keyMyths.title: Creation of the First World
keyMyths.description: Tawa, along with Spider Woman, created the first people from clay, bringing them to life with the warmth of the sun and his breath. This marked the beginning of life on Earth according to Hopi tradition.
keyMyths.source: Waters, Frank. Book of the Hopi.

keyMyths.title: Tawa's Daily Journey
keyMyths.description: Each day, Tawa travels across the sky, bringing light and warmth to the world. This journey symbolizes the cycle of life, death, and rebirth, and is essential for the sustenance of all living things.
keyMyths.source: Oswalt, Wendell H. This Land Was Theirs: A Study of the North American Indian.

keyMyths.title: The Importance of Sunlight
keyMyths.description: Tawa's sunlight is vital for the growth of crops and the survival of the Hopi people in their arid environment. Prayers and ceremonies are performed to honor Tawa and ensure the continuation of this life-giving force.
keyMyths.source: Fewkes, Jesse Walter. Hopi Katcinas Drawn by Native Artists.

sources.author: Waters, Frank
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Hopi mythology and culture.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Hopi mythology and culture.

sources.author: Oswalt, Wendell H.
